--- SOURCE 1: Immune Responses: Primary and Secondary - Wiley Online Library ---
URL: https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/9780470015902.a0029196

SUMMARY:
Immune responses to antigens may be categorised as primary or secondary responses. The primary immune response to antigen occurs on the first occasion it is encountered. This response can take up to 14 days to resolve and leads to the generation of memory cells with a high specificity for the inducing antigen.

--- IMAGES ---

IMAGE 1:
URL: https://microbenotes.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Primary-Immune-Response-vs-Secondary-Immune-response-768x501.jpeg
DESCRIPTION: The graph illustrates the antibody concentration over time, comparing the primary immune response to an initial exposure to antigen A with the secondary immune response resulting from re-exposure to antigen A and initial exposure to antigen B.

IMAGE 2:
URL: https://microbenotes.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Immune-Response.jpeg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the immune response mechanism targeting microbial pathogens and macroparasites, highlighting the destruction of pathogens, elimination of infected cells, and expulsion of noxious substances.

IMAGE 3:
URL: https://i.pinimg.com/736x/41/eb/5d/41eb5d74d6faf02a1a52e4331aa4d65b.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the progression and characteristics of the primary and secondary antibody responses over time after antigen exposure, highlighting the roles of naive B cells, activated B cells, plasma cells, and memory B cells in producing different types of antibodies (IgM, IgG) and the duration of antibody production.

IMAGE 4:
URL: https://biologynotesonline.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/10/ggggg-1024x576.jpg
DESCRIPTION: The image illustrates the processes and key components involved in the primary and secondary immune responses, including the activation of B cells, formation of memory cells, and the role of macrophages in pathogen presentation.

IMAGE 5:
URL: https://biologynotesonline.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/image-1682.png
DESCRIPTION: The illustration depicts the progression from primary to secondary immune responses over time, highlighting key processes such as activation of B cells, antibody secretion, and the formation of long-lived plasma and memory B cells following initial and subsequent antigen exposures.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The initial encounter with an antigen activates the immune system, triggering a complex response that is slower than subsequent exposures, taking about 5-14 days to develop.

enriched_points.point_title: Activation and proliferation of immune cells
enriched_points.expanded_content: Upon first exposure, naïve T and B cells are activated, proliferating and differentiating into effector cells and memory cells, which provide long-term immunity.
The primary immune response is characterized by a slower onset and predominance of IgM antibodies, resulting in a lower overall magnitude compared to secondary responses.
The primary immune response can last weeks, with detectable antibodies peaking around 10-14 days post-exposure, before gradually declining as the immune system returns to baseline.
Booster doses are crucial as they stimulate memory cells, enhancing antibody production and ensuring a robust and quicker response upon re-exposure to the antigen.
